subtasks.title: Implement TAP interface creation in src/tap.rs with ioctl TUNSETIFF
subtasks.description: Implement create_tap_interface function using nix::libc::ioctl with TUNSETIFF request, IFF_TAP|IFF_NO_PI flags to create TAP network interfaces and return the RawFd for FD passing
subtasks.details: In crates/eoip-helper/src/tap.rs: (1) Import required items: use std::os::unix::io::{RawFd, AsRawFd}; use std::ffi::CString; use nix::libc::{ioctl, open, O_RDWR, IFF_TAP, IFF_NO_PI, TUNSETIFF, IFNAMSIZ}; use nix::errno::Errno; use eoip_proto::error::EoipError;. (2) Define ifreq struct matching Linux kernel layout: #[repr(C)] struct ifreq { ifr_name: [u8; IFNAMSIZ], ifr_flags: i16, _padding: [u8; 22] }. (3) Implement pub fn create_tap_interface(name: &str) -> Result<RawFd, EoipError> that: validates name.len() < IFNAMSIZ-1 (return TapError if too long), opens /dev/net/tun with O_RDWR (return TapError on failure), creates ifreq with name copied and ifr_flags = IFF_TAP | IFF_NO_PI, calls unsafe { ioctl(fd, TUNSETIFF, &ifreq) } (return TapError if -1), extracts actual interface name from ifreq.ifr_name (null-terminated), logs created interface name with tracing::info!, returns fd as RawFd. (4) Add comprehensive doc comment explaining TUNSETIFF creates virtual network device, IFF_TAP sets Layer 2 mode (Ethernet frames), IFF_NO_PI disables packet info header for performance. (5) Document that caller owns returned fd and must close it or pass to daemon. (6) Add helper function get_ifreq_name(ifreq: &ifreq) -> String to safely extract null-terminated name. (7) Add inline comments explaining kernel interface expectations and error conditions.
subtasks.testStrategy: Unit tests (require root or CAP_NET_ADMIN): (1) create_tap_interface("eoip0") returns valid fd, verify with 'ip link show eoip0' (test must clean up via ioctl TUNSETIFF delete or ip link del). (2) Test with name exactly IFNAMSIZ-1 characters succeeds. (3) Test with name IFNAMSIZ characters returns TapError. (4) Test with empty name returns error. (5) Test without root returns permission denied TapError. (6) Integration test: create TAP, write raw Ethernet frame, verify readable from other end (requires root). Mock test: verify ifreq struct layout matches kernel with offset assertions. Document that full tests require root privileges or CI container with CAP_NET_ADMIN.

subtasks.title: Implement raw socket creation in src/rawsock.rs for IPv4/IPv6 protocols
subtasks.description: Implement create_raw_socket_v4 and create_raw_socket_v6 functions using socket2 crate to create raw sockets for IP protocol 47 (GRE/EoIP) and protocol 97 (EtherIP/EoIPv6) with proper error handling and socket option configuration
subtasks.details: In crates/eoip-helper/src/rawsock.rs: (1) Import: use std::os::unix::io::{RawFd, IntoRawFd}; use socket2::{Socket, Domain, Type, Protocol}; use eoip_proto::error::EoipError;. (2) Implement pub fn create_raw_socket_v4() -> Result<RawFd, EoipError> that: creates Socket::new(Domain::IPV4, Type::RAW, Some(Protocol::from(47)))? where 47 is GRE protocol number, maps socket2::Error to EoipError::RawSocketError via ?, sets socket options: set_nonblocking(true)? for async I/O compatibility, optionally set_reuse_address(true)? and set_reuse_port(true)? per design requirements, logs socket creation with tracing::info!("Created raw socket for IPv4 protocol 47 (EoIP)"), returns socket.into_raw_fd(). (3) Implement pub fn create_raw_socket_v6() -> Result<RawFd, EoipError> that: creates Socket::new(Domain::IPV6, Type::RAW, Some(Protocol::from(97)))? where 97 is EtherIP protocol number per RFC 3378, sets IPV6_V6ONLY socket option to true via set_only_v6(true)? to prevent IPv4-mapped IPv6 addresses, sets nonblocking(true), logs socket creation with tracing::info!("Created raw socket for IPv6 protocol 97 (EtherIP)"), returns socket.into_raw_fd(). (4) Add comprehensive doc comments explaining: protocol 47 is GRE used for MikroTik EoIP over IPv4, protocol 97 is RFC 3378 EtherIP used for EoIPv6 over IPv6, raw sockets require CAP_NET_RAW capability or root, nonblocking mode required for tokio/async integration. (5) Document socket ownership transfer via into_raw_fd(). (6) Add error context with map_err to include protocol number in error messages.
subtasks.testStrategy: Unit tests (require root or CAP_NET_RAW): (1) create_raw_socket_v4() returns valid fd, verify socket exists via /proc/self/fd inspection. (2) create_raw_socket_v6() returns valid fd. (3) Test without CAP_NET_RAW returns permission denied error. (4) Verify nonblocking mode: attempt blocking read with timeout=0, should return EAGAIN immediately. (5) Verify IPV6_V6ONLY on v6 socket by attempting to bind IPv4-mapped address (should fail). (6) Integration test: create socket, bind to source IP, send crafted GRE packet, verify sendto() succeeds (requires root). (7) Test socket options are correctly set by reading back via getsockopt. Document tests require root or CI with CAP_NET_RAW. Mock tests verify error handling paths without requiring privileges.

subtasks.title: Implement FD passing via SCM_RIGHTS in src/fdpass.rs and privilege dropping in src/privdrop.rs
subtasks.description: Implement send_fd_via_scm_rights function using nix::sys::socket::sendmsg with ControlMessage::ScmRights to pass file descriptors over Unix domain sockets, and implement drop_privileges function calling setgid/setuid for security hardening after resource creation
subtasks.details: In crates/eoip-helper/src/fdpass.rs: (1) Import: use std::os::unix::io::RawFd; use nix::sys::socket::{sendmsg, ControlMessage, MsgFlags}; use nix::sys::uio::IoVec; use eoip_proto::wire::HelperMsg; use eoip_proto::error::EoipError;. (2) Implement pub fn send_fd_via_scm_rights(unix_sock: RawFd, fd_to_send: RawFd, msg: &HelperMsg) -> Result<(), EoipError> that: serializes msg using bincode::serialize (add bincode dev-dependency), creates IoVec::from_slice(&serialized_bytes), creates ControlMessage::ScmRights(&[fd_to_send]), calls sendmsg(unix_sock, &[iov], &[cmsg], MsgFlags::empty(), None)?, maps nix::Error to EoipError::HelperDisconnected, logs FD sent with tracing::debug!("Sent fd={fd_to_send} with message {msg:?}"), returns Ok(()). (3) Add doc comment explaining SCM_RIGHTS is POSIX ancillary data mechanism for passing file descriptors between processes, kernel translates fd numbers during transfer, receiving process gets different fd number referencing same kernel object. Document message format: serialized HelperMsg in IoVec, fd in ancillary data. In crates/eoip-helper/src/privdrop.rs: (1) Import: use nix::unistd::{setgid, setuid, Gid, Uid, getuid, getgid}; use eoip_proto::error::EoipError;. (2) Implement pub fn drop_privileges(target_uid: u32, target_gid: u32) -> Result<(), EoipError> that: validates current uid == 0 (root) via getuid().is_root(), return error if not root, calls setgid(Gid::from_raw(target_gid))? first (GID must be dropped before UID per POSIX), then setuid(Uid::from_raw(target_uid))?, verifies drop succeeded: getuid() == target_uid && getgid() == target_gid, logs privilege drop with tracing::warn!("Dropped privileges to uid={target_uid} gid={target_gid}"), returns Ok(()). (3) Add SECURITY comment explaining order matters: setgid before setuid, irreversible operation, verification required. (4) Document use case: MODE_PERSIST calls this after initial setup, MODE_EXIT doesn't need it (process exits).
subtasks.testStrategy: FD passing tests: (1) Create Unix socketpair, send dummy fd (e.g., open /dev/null), verify receiver gets valid fd via recvmsg with SCM_RIGHTS (requires separate recv implementation or mock). (2) Test serialization roundtrip: HelperMsg::TapCreated{iface_name:"eoip0".into(), tunnel_id:100} serializes and attaches to correct IoVec. (3) Test error handling: closed socket returns HelperDisconnected. Privilege drop tests (require root): (1) Start as root, drop_privileges(1000, 1000), verify getuid()==1000 && getgid()==1000. (2) Verify cannot regain root after drop (setuid(0) fails with EPERM). (3) Test calling drop_privileges when not root returns error. (4) Test wrong order (setuid before setgid in implementation) caught in code review. (5) Integration test: helper creates TAP as root, sends via SCM_RIGHTS, drops to user, attempts to create another TAP (should fail). Document root requirement for full test suite.

subtasks.title: Implement main.rs with CLI parsing, Unix socket listener, and MODE_PERSIST/MODE_EXIT logic
subtasks.description: Implement the main entry point with clap-based CLI argument parsing for --mode (persist|exit) and --socket-path, establish Unix domain socket connection with daemon, implement main loop listening for DaemonMsg commands (CreateTunnel, Shutdown), and coordinate resource creation with FD passing for both operational modes
subtasks.details: In crates/eoip-helper/src/main.rs: (1) Define #[derive(Parser)] struct Args { #[arg(long, value_enum)] mode: Mode, #[arg(long, default_value="/var/run/eoip/helper.sock")] socket_path: String }. (2) Define #[derive(ValueEnum, Clone)] enum Mode { Persist, Exit }. (3) In main() -> Result<(), EoipError>: parse Args via Args::parse(), initialize tracing_subscriber with env filter (RUST_LOG), log startup with info!("eoip-helper starting in {:?} mode", args.mode), connect to Unix socket at args.socket_path using std::os::unix::net::UnixStream::connect()?, get unix_sock RawFd via as_raw_fd(). (4) Match on args.mode: For Mode::Exit: read initial config from socket (expect DaemonMsg), create all resources (TAP interfaces per config, raw sockets v4/v6), send each via send_fd_via_scm_rights with corresponding HelperMsg (TapCreated, RawSocket), send HelperMsg::HelperReady when done, exit process with std::process::exit(0). For Mode::Persist: send HelperMsg::HelperReady immediately, enter loop with nix::sys::socket::recvmsg to read DaemonMsg from unix_sock, match msg: DaemonMsg::CreateTunnel{iface_name, tunnel_id} => create_tap_interface(&iface_name)?, create raw sockets if first tunnel, send_fd_via_scm_rights(), DaemonMsg::Shutdown => break loop and exit cleanly. (5) Add comprehensive error handling: socket connection failures, ioctl failures, permission errors, serialize errors. (6) Add signal handlers for SIGTERM/SIGINT in PERSIST mode for clean shutdown. (7) Log all operations at appropriate levels: info for lifecycle events, debug for FD operations, error for failures. (8) Add doc comments explaining two modes: EXIT for static config (helper creates all, exits, minimal attack surface), PERSIST for dynamic tunnels (helper stays alive, responds to daemon requests, allows runtime tunnel addition). (9) Document that PERSIST mode should call drop_privileges after creating shared resources but validation shows this conflicts with per-tunnel TAP creation requiring root - note this design trade-off in comments.
subtasks.testStrategy: Integration tests (require root): (1) MODE_EXIT test: start helper, send mock config via Unix socket, verify receives TapCreated + RawSocket messages with valid fds, verify helper exits. (2) MODE_PERSIST test: start helper, send CreateTunnel message, verify receives TapCreated with valid fd, verify helper still running, send Shutdown, verify clean exit. (3) Test CLI parsing: --mode persist --socket-path /tmp/test.sock parses correctly. (4) Test socket connection failure (path doesn't exist) returns appropriate error. (5) Test invalid DaemonMsg in MODE_EXIT returns error. (6) Test signal handling: send SIGTERM in PERSIST mode, verify clean shutdown. (7) Stress test PERSIST mode: send 100 CreateTunnel requests sequentially, verify all succeed. (8) Test error recovery: CreateTunnel with invalid iface name, verify error response sent, helper remains operational. (9) End-to-end test: start helper and mock daemon on opposite ends of socketpair, create tunnel, verify fd transfer, verify TAP interface usable by daemon. Document tests require root and Unix socket mocking framework.

tasks.title: Implement daemon core infrastructure with config, tunnel registry, and FD receiver
tasks.description: Build eoip-rs daemon foundation: TOML config parsing, DashMap-based tunnel registry, FD receiver for helper integration, lifecycle state machine, graceful shutdown
tasks.details:
Create crates/eoip-rs with Cargo.toml workspace deps. Implement:
- src/config.rs: #[derive(Deserialize)] structs for Config{daemon,api,logging,performance,tunnels}, TunnelConfig{tunnel_id,local,remote,iface_name,mtu,enabled,keepalive}, parse_config(path: &Path)
- src/tunnel/registry.rs: TunnelRegistry wrapping DashMap<DemuxKey, Arc<TunnelHandle>>, methods insert/remove/get/iter
- src/tunnel/handle.rs: TunnelHandle{tap_fd: Arc<AsyncFd<OwnedFd>>, stats: Arc<TunnelStats>, state: Arc<AtomicU8>, keepalive_tx}
- src/tunnel/lifecycle.rs: TunnelState enum (Initializing=0, Configured=1, Active=2, Stale=3, TearingDown=4, Destroyed=5) with AtomicU8 backing
- src/net/fdrecv.rs: recv_fd_via_scm_rights(unix_sock) returning (RawFd, HelperMsg) using nix::sys::socket::recvmsg
- src/shutdown.rs: ShutdownCoordinator with tokio_util::sync::CancellationToken, signal handler for SIGTERM/SIGINT
- src/main.rs: Load config, connect to helper socket, spawn FD receiver task, initialize TunnelRegistry

Use tracing macros (info!, warn!, error!) throughout.
tasks.testStrategy: Config parsing tests: valid TOML→struct, invalid→error. Registry concurrency test: 1000 parallel inserts/lookups. Lifecycle FSM test: valid transitions only. Shutdown test: CancellationToken triggers task cleanup. Mock helper socket for FD receiver

subtasks.title: Create eoip-rs crate structure and implement TOML configuration parser
subtasks.description: Set up the crates/eoip-rs directory with Cargo.toml and implement src/config.rs with TOML deserialization for daemon configuration including tunnel definitions, API settings, logging, and performance parameters
subtasks.details: Create crates/eoip-rs/Cargo.toml with workspace dependencies (tokio, serde, toml, tracing, dashmap, nix, crossbeam). Implement src/config.rs with: (1) Config struct containing daemon: DaemonConfig, api: ApiConfig, logging: LoggingConfig, performance: PerformanceConfig, tunnels: Vec<TunnelConfig>; (2) DaemonConfig with fields user, group, helper_mode ("persist"/"exit"), helper_socket (PathBuf), pid_file (PathBuf); (3) TunnelConfig with tunnel_id: u16, local: IpAddr, remote: IpAddr, iface_name: Option<String>, mtu: u16, enabled: bool, keepalive: KeepaliveConfig; (4) KeepaliveConfig with interval: Duration, timeout: Duration; (5) parse_config(path: &Path) -> Result<Config, ConfigError> function using toml::from_str. Add #[derive(Debug, Clone, Deserialize)] to all config structs. Use thiserror for ConfigError enum with variants for IoError, ParseError, ValidationError.
subtasks.testStrategy: Unit tests in src/config.rs: (1) parse_config with valid example TOML matching docs/design/architecture.md format, verify all fields deserialize correctly; (2) invalid TOML triggers ParseError; (3) missing required fields trigger appropriate errors; (4) tunnel_id > 4095 triggers ValidationError; (5) test with both IPv4 and IPv6 local/remote addresses; (6) verify Duration parsing from "10s", "30s" format works correctly

subtasks.title: Implement tunnel lifecycle state machine and handle structures
subtasks.description: Create the tunnel state management infrastructure with atomic state transitions, tunnel handle wrapper containing TAP FD and stats, and lifecycle state enum matching the FSM specification
subtasks.details: Implement src/tunnel/mod.rs as module root. Create src/tunnel/lifecycle.rs with: (1) TunnelState enum with variants Initializing = 0, Configured = 1, Active = 2, Stale = 3, TearingDown = 4, Destroyed = 5; (2) impl for TunnelState with to_u8() -> u8 and from_u8(u8) -> Option<Self>; (3) transition validation function is_valid_transition(from: TunnelState, to: TunnelState) -> bool implementing FSM from architecture.md. Create src/tunnel/handle.rs with: (1) TunnelHandle struct { tap_fd: Arc<AsyncFd<OwnedFd>>, stats: Arc<TunnelStats>, state: Arc<AtomicU8>, keepalive_tx: mpsc::Sender<KeepaliveEvent> }; (2) TunnelStats struct from eoip-proto types with AtomicU64 fields for rx_packets, rx_bytes, tx_packets, tx_bytes, rx_errors, tx_errors, last_rx: AtomicI64, last_tx: AtomicI64; (3) KeepaliveEvent enum for keepalive state machine messages. Use tokio::io::unix::AsyncFd and std::os::fd::OwnedFd.
subtasks.testStrategy: Unit tests: (1) TunnelState roundtrip through to_u8/from_u8 for all variants; (2) is_valid_transition accepts Initializing→Configured, Configured→Active, Active→Stale, Stale→Active, Active→TearingDown, etc.; (3) is_valid_transition rejects invalid transitions like Destroyed→Active, Initializing→TearingDown; (4) TunnelStats atomic operations are Send+Sync verified via compilation; (5) verify AtomicU8 state can be loaded/stored correctly; (6) test concurrent stat updates from multiple threads using Arc<TunnelStats>

subtasks.title: Implement DashMap-based tunnel registry with concurrent access patterns
subtasks.description: Build the TunnelRegistry structure wrapping DashMap for O(1) tunnel lookups by (tunnel_id, peer_addr) key with thread-safe insert/remove/get operations for the RX demux path
subtasks.details: Create src/tunnel/registry.rs implementing: (1) TunnelRegistry struct wrapping DashMap<DemuxKey, Arc<TunnelHandle>> where DemuxKey is imported from eoip-proto::types; (2) impl TunnelRegistry with methods: new() -> Self, insert(key: DemuxKey, handle: Arc<TunnelHandle>) -> Option<Arc<TunnelHandle>>, remove(key: &DemuxKey) -> Option<Arc<TunnelHandle>>, get(key: &DemuxKey) -> Option<Arc<TunnelHandle>> using DashMap's get() which returns a reference guard, len() -> usize, iter() returning iterator over entries; (3) Add helper method find_by_tunnel_id(tid: u16) -> Vec<(DemuxKey, Arc<TunnelHandle>)> for listing all tunnels with given ID across different peers. Use dashmap::DashMap from workspace dependencies. Ensure all methods work correctly with Arc<TunnelHandle> to avoid cloning the underlying data. Add tracing::debug! logs for insert/remove operations.
subtasks.testStrategy: Concurrency test: spawn 1000 parallel tasks, half inserting random DemuxKey entries with unique (tunnel_id, IpAddr) pairs, half performing get() lookups, verify no panics and all inserts are retrievable. Collision test: insert same DemuxKey twice, verify second insert returns the old value. Iteration test: insert 100 entries, verify iter() yields all 100 with correct handles. Remove test: insert, then remove, verify get() returns None. find_by_tunnel_id test: insert tunnels with same tunnel_id but different peer IPs, verify all are found. Run tests with LOOM for memory ordering verification if feasible.

subtasks.title: Implement FD receiver using Unix domain socket SCM_RIGHTS for helper integration
subtasks.description: Create the file descriptor receiving infrastructure using SCM_RIGHTS ancillary messages to receive TAP and raw socket FDs from the privileged eoip-helper process over Unix domain sockets
subtasks.details: Create src/net/mod.rs and src/net/fdrecv.rs implementing: (1) recv_fd_via_scm_rights(unix_sock: &UnixStream) -> Result<(RawFd, HelperMsg), FdRecvError> using nix::sys::socket::recvmsg with ControlMessageOwned::ScmRights parsing; (2) HelperMsg enum from eoip-proto wire protocol with variants TapCreated { iface_name: String, mtu: u16 }, RawSocketCreated { family: AddressFamily }, Error { message: String }; (3) Deserialize HelperMsg from the socket's regular data buffer (use bincode or similar); (4) Convert received RawFd to OwnedFd using std::os::fd::OwnedFd::from; (5) Error handling with FdRecvError enum covering socket errors, missing SCM_RIGHTS, invalid message format, helper errors. Add comprehensive tracing with info! when FD received, error! on failures. Use nix 0.29 from workspace dependencies with socket and user features.
subtasks.testStrategy: Mock integration test: create UnixStream pair via UnixStream::pair(), send SCM_RIGHTS message from one end with a dummy FD (e.g., /dev/null), verify recv_fd_via_scm_rights receives it correctly. HelperMsg deserialization test: serialize each HelperMsg variant, send over socket, verify correct deserialization. Error path test: call recv_fd_via_scm_rights on socket without SCM_RIGHTS, verify FdRecvError returned. Test with both TAP and raw socket message types. Note: Full end-to-end testing requires actual eoip-helper binary (deferred to integration tests).

subtasks.title: Implement graceful shutdown coordinator and integrate main daemon entry point
subtasks.description: Build the shutdown coordination system using CancellationToken for signal propagation, implement signal handlers for SIGTERM/SIGINT, and create main.rs that initializes all components, loads config, connects to helper, and runs the daemon lifecycle
subtasks.details: Create src/shutdown.rs implementing: (1) ShutdownCoordinator struct { token: CancellationToken, shutdown_complete_tx: mpsc::Sender<()> } using tokio_util::sync::CancellationToken; (2) Methods: new() -> Self, shutdown_token() -> CancellationToken (clone of internal token), shutdown() triggering token.cancel(); (3) setup_signal_handlers(coordinator: Arc<ShutdownCoordinator>) spawning tokio task that awaits tokio::signal::ctrl_c() and signal::unix::signal(SignalKind::terminate()), calling coordinator.shutdown() on signal. Create src/main.rs with: (1) #[tokio::main] async fn main() -> Result<()>; (2) tracing_subscriber initialization with env filter from config.logging.level; (3) parse_config() from CLI args (use clap with --config flag); (4) Connect to helper socket at config.daemon.helper_socket using tokio::net::UnixStream; (5) Initialize TunnelRegistry; (6) Spawn FD receiver task in background; (7) Setup ShutdownCoordinator and signal handlers; (8) Create tokio::select! loop awaiting shutdown_token.cancelled(); (9) On shutdown, log graceful shutdown message, drop registry, wait for tasks. Use tracing macros throughout: info! for startup, warn! for signals, error! for failures.
subtasks.testStrategy: Shutdown token test: create ShutdownCoordinator, clone token, call shutdown(), verify token.is_cancelled() returns true. Signal handler test (requires tokio_test): send SIGTERM to test process, verify shutdown() is called (use mock or inspect logs). Integration test: run minimal main() with test config pointing to non-existent helper socket, verify graceful error handling and shutdown. Config loading test: create valid/invalid TOML files, verify main() handles both cases correctly. Verify tracing output contains expected log levels. Test that shutdown cancels spawned tasks correctly by creating a test task that awaits the cancellation token.

tasks.title: Implement RX packet path with raw socket demultiplexing and TAP delivery
tasks.description: Build high-performance RX pipeline: dedicated OS thread with recvmmsg, header parsing, DashMap-based demux, crossbeam channel to TAP writer, adaptive batching
tasks.details:
Implement:
- src/packet/rx.rs: spawn_rx_worker_v4(raw_sock: RawFd, registry: Arc<TunnelRegistry>, buffer_pool) spawning std::thread with recvmmsg loop (batch size 64), parse headers via eoip_proto, lookup DemuxKey in registry, send to tunnel's crossbeam channel
- spawn_rx_worker_v6 for IPv6/proto 97
- Per-tunnel consumer task: recv from crossbeam::channel, write to TAP via AsyncFd<OwnedFd>, update stats.rx_packets/rx_bytes atomically
- src/net/tap.rs: TapAsyncFd wrapping AsyncFd<OwnedFd> with async read/write methods
- Buffer pool: crossbeam::queue::ArrayQueue<PacketBuf> where PacketBuf has 64B headroom + 1522B payload, impl Drop to return to pool

Use nix::sys::socket::recvmmsg with iovec array. Handle EAGAIN gracefully. Log demux misses at INFO level with rate limiting (e.g., max 1/sec).
tasks.testStrategy: Benchmark test: synthetic packet generator feeding raw socket, measure demux throughput (target 500K pps). Correctness test: send crafted EoIP packet, verify TAP receives exact Ethernet frame. Stress test: 100 concurrent tunnels, verify no drops. Buffer pool leak test: verify all buffers returned after 1M packets

subtasks.title: Implement zero-copy buffer pool with PacketBuf and headroom management
subtasks.description: Create the buffer pool infrastructure using crossbeam::queue::ArrayQueue<PacketBuf> with 64-byte headroom + 1522-byte payload layout for zero-copy header prepending, implementing Drop trait for automatic buffer return
subtasks.details: Create src/packet/mod.rs and src/packet/buffer.rs implementing: (1) PacketBuf struct with fields: data: Box<[u8]> (total size 1586 bytes = 64 headroom + 1522 max frame), head: usize (start of valid data), tail: usize (end of valid data), pool: Weak<BufferPool> (for Drop return). (2) BufferPool struct wrapping ArrayQueue<PacketBuf> with configurable pool size (default 4096 buffers based on performance.md formula: max(256, rx_batch_size * 4 + max_tunnels * 2)). (3) Methods: BufferPool::new(pool_size: usize) -> Arc<Self>, get() -> Option<PacketBuf> (pop from queue, reset head/tail), put(buf: PacketBuf) (push back to queue). (4) PacketBuf methods: payload_ptr() -> *mut u8 returning &mut data[head], capacity() -> usize returning tail-head, set_payload(offset: usize, len: usize) for setting valid data range after recvmmsg, prepend_header(header_len: usize) for TX path shifting head backward to write headers in headroom. (5) impl Drop for PacketBuf calling pool.upgrade().map(|p| p.put(self)) to return buffer on drop. (6) Constants: HEADER_HEADROOM = 64, MAX_FRAME_SIZE = 1522, BUF_TOTAL = 1586. Use crossbeam::queue::ArrayQueue for lock-free MPMC access. Add tracing::warn! when pool exhausted (drop packet rather than allocate). Document memory layout with ASCII diagram matching performance.md section 4.
subtasks.testStrategy: Unit tests: (1) Create pool with size 16, get 16 buffers, verify 17th returns None (exhaustion). (2) Get buffer, drop it, verify returned to pool (get again succeeds). (3) Verify buffer layout: data.len() == 1586, initial head == 64, tail == 1586. (4) Test prepend_header: buf with head=64, prepend_header(8), verify head == 56. (5) Concurrency test: spawn 8 threads, each performing 10000 get/put cycles, verify no panics and final pool count == initial. (6) Memory leak test: create pool, perform 100000 get/drop cycles, verify pool size stable (no leaks). (7) Test payload_ptr points to correct offset in data array. Run with LOOM for memory ordering verification.

subtasks.title: Implement dedicated RX worker thread with recvmmsg batch receive loop
subtasks.description: Create spawn_rx_worker_v4 and spawn_rx_worker_v6 functions that spawn OS threads running tight recvmmsg loops (batch size 64) on raw sockets, parsing IP headers to extract source addresses and payloads for demultiplexing
subtasks.details: Create src/packet/rx.rs with: (1) spawn_rx_worker_v4(raw_sock: RawFd, registry: Arc<TunnelRegistry>, buffer_pool: Arc<BufferPool>) -> JoinHandle<()> spawning std::thread::spawn (NOT tokio task) running rx_loop_v4. (2) rx_loop_v4 implementation: allocate stack arrays const BATCH: usize = 64; let mut iovecs: [iovec; BATCH], msgs: [mmsghdr; BATCH], addrs: [sockaddr_in; BATCH], pre-wire iovec→buffer, msghdr→iovec+addr per performance.md pseudocode. (3) Main loop: call nix::sys::socket::recvmmsg(raw_sock, &mut msgs, BATCH, MsgFlags::MSG_DONTWAIT, None), handle n <= 0 with EAGAIN using nix::poll::poll with POLLIN to wait for readability (avoid busy-wait), continue on other errors after logging. (4) Process received packets: for i in 0..n, extract len = msgs[i].msg_len, src_ip from addrs[i].sin_addr, parse IPv4 header to get ip_hdr_len = (buf.data[0] & 0x0F) * 4 (IP header IHL field), skip to GRE payload at offset ip_hdr_len. (5) Call eoip_proto::validate_and_parse_eoip(&buf.data[ip_hdr_len..]) to get (tunnel_id, payload_offset), construct DemuxKey { tunnel_id, peer_addr: IpAddr::V4(src_ip) }. (6) Lookup in registry.get(&key), on hit: extract Ethernet frame starting at ip_hdr_len + payload_offset, update buf.set_payload(), send to tunnel via handle.rx_tx.try_send(buf) (crossbeam bounded channel), on miss: log demux miss at INFO with rate limiting (e.g., std::sync::atomic counter + timestamp check, max 1/sec), drop packet. (7) Replenish consumed buffers from pool after each batch. (8) Implement spawn_rx_worker_v6 with same pattern using sockaddr_in6 and IPv6 header parsing (NextHeader field at offset 6, fixed 40-byte header), calling validate_and_parse_eoipv6. Use nix 0.29 recvmmsg API. Handle EWOULDBLOCK/EAGAIN gracefully with polling. Add tracing::error! for unexpected socket errors, tracing::info_span! for thread context.
subtasks.testStrategy: Mock socket test: create Unix socketpair as raw_fd substitute, send crafted EoIP packet matching protocol.md section 12.1 (TID=42, 64-byte frame), verify RX worker parses correctly and sends to mock channel. Batch test: send 64 packets rapidly, verify recvmmsg receives all in one syscall (check logs/instrumentation). Demux miss test: send packet with unknown tunnel_id, verify logged at INFO with correct rate limiting (send 1000 in 100ms, verify max ~100 log lines). IPv4 header parsing test: craft packets with IHL=5 (20 bytes) and IHL=15 (60 bytes with options), verify correct payload offset. Performance benchmark: synthetic packet generator feeding socketpair at 1 Mpps, measure RX worker throughput and CPU usage. Stress test: run 1M packets through worker, verify buffer pool stable (no leaks). Verify thread does not block tokio runtime.

subtasks.title: Implement per-tunnel TAP writer consumer tasks with AsyncFd integration
subtasks.description: Create spawn_tap_writer_task function that spawns tokio async tasks for each tunnel, receiving PacketBuf from crossbeam channel and writing Ethernet frames to TAP device via AsyncFd<OwnedFd>, updating RX statistics atomically
subtasks.details: In src/packet/rx.rs add: (1) spawn_tap_writer_task(tap_fd: Arc<AsyncFd<OwnedFd>>, rx_channel: crossbeam::channel::Receiver<PacketBuf>, stats: Arc<TunnelStats>, shutdown_token: CancellationToken) -> JoinHandle<Result<(), IoError>> spawning tokio::spawn async task. (2) Task loop: tokio::select! { _ = shutdown_token.cancelled() => break (graceful shutdown), buf = tokio::task::spawn_blocking(move || rx_channel.recv()) => process buffer }. Use spawn_blocking because crossbeam::Receiver::recv() is blocking and not async. (3) On buffer receive: loop { match tap_fd.writable().await { Ok(ready_guard) => { match ready_guard.try_io(|fd| { let n = nix::unistd::write(fd.as_raw_fd(), &buf.data[buf.head..buf.tail])?; Ok(n) }) { Ok(Ok(n)) => { stats.rx_packets.fetch_add(1, Ordering::Relaxed); stats.rx_bytes.fetch_add(n as u64, Ordering::Relaxed); stats.last_rx.store(now_millis(), Ordering::Relaxed); break; // success }, Ok(Err(e)) if e == Errno::EWOULDBLOCK => continue, // retry writable().await, Ok(Err(e)) => { stats.rx_errors.fetch_add(1, Ordering::Relaxed); tracing::warn!("TAP write error: {}", e); break; // drop packet }, Err(_would_block) => continue, // AsyncFd not ready, retry } }, Err(e) => { tracing::error!("TAP AsyncFd error: {}", e); break; } } }. (4) After write, drop(buf) returns buffer to pool automatically. (5) Helper function now_millis() -> i64 returning SystemTime::now().duration_since(UNIX_EPOCH).as_millis() as i64. (6) Use std::os::fd::AsRawFd to get RawFd from OwnedFd. Add comprehensive error handling for EAGAIN (queue full, TAP buffer full), EIO (TAP device gone - trigger tunnel teardown), ENOBUFS (drop and log). Update TunnelHandle in src/tunnel/handle.rs to include rx_channel: crossbeam::channel::Sender<PacketBuf> that RX worker sends to.
subtasks.testStrategy: Integration test: create mock TAP fd pair using Unix socketpair, spawn writer task, send PacketBuf via channel with known Ethernet frame, verify exact bytes written to socket. Statistics test: send 100 packets, verify stats.rx_packets == 100, rx_bytes == sum of frame sizes, last_rx timestamp updated. Error handling test: close TAP fd, send packet, verify rx_errors incremented and task doesn't panic. Backpressure test: fill TAP buffer (send large packets faster than consumed), verify EWOULDBLOCK handling with writable().await retry. Shutdown test: send cancellation token, verify task exits cleanly and remaining channel packets are drained or dropped. Channel capacity test: send 1000 packets to bounded channel (capacity 512), verify 488 dropped gracefully if RX thread overruns. Buffer return test: verify buffers returned to pool after write (check pool size before/after).

subtasks.title: Create AsyncFd TAP device wrapper with async read/write methods
subtasks.description: Implement src/net/tap.rs with TapAsyncFd struct wrapping AsyncFd<OwnedFd> for TAP interfaces, providing async-friendly read/write methods for integration with tokio runtime while maintaining non-blocking semantics
subtasks.details: Create src/net/tap.rs with: (1) TapAsyncFd struct { inner: AsyncFd<OwnedFd> } wrapping the TAP file descriptor in tokio's AsyncFd for epoll-based async I/O. (2) impl TapAsyncFd { pub fn new(fd: OwnedFd) -> io::Result<Self> creating AsyncFd::new(fd)? and returning Self { inner }. pub async fn read(&self, buf: &mut [u8]) -> io::Result<usize> that: loop { match self.inner.readable().await? { ready_guard => match ready_guard.try_io(|fd| nix::unistd::read(fd.as_raw_fd(), buf)) { Ok(Ok(n)) => return Ok(n), Ok(Err(e)) if e == Errno::EWOULDBLOCK => continue, // retry readable().await, Ok(Err(e)) => return Err(io::Error::from_raw_os_error(e as i32)), Err(_would_block) => continue, // AsyncFd not ready yet } } }. pub async fn write(&self, buf: &[u8]) -> io::Result<usize> with symmetric logic using writable().await and nix::unistd::write. pub fn into_shared(self) -> Arc<Self> wrapping in Arc for sharing across tasks. }. (3) Re-export in src/net/mod.rs as pub use tap::TapAsyncFd;. (4) Add comprehensive doc comments explaining AsyncFd provides async/await interface over non-blocking fd using epoll, IFF_NO_PI mode means read/write operate on raw Ethernet frames without packet info header. Document that read() may return frames up to MTU size (1500+14+4 = 1518 bytes typical, 1522 with VLAN tag). Add safety notes about OwnedFd ownership transfer.
subtasks.testStrategy: Unit tests: (1) Create TapAsyncFd from mock fd (Unix socket), test read() with AsyncFd mocking. (2) Test write() similarly. (3) Test EWOULDBLOCK retry behavior by forcing writable()/readable() to return WouldBlock initially. Integration test (requires root): create real TAP device via eoip-helper, wrap in TapAsyncFd, write Ethernet frame, verify readable on other end via ip tuntap commands or tcpdump. Concurrency test: share Arc<TapAsyncFd> across 10 tasks, each reading/writing, verify no data corruption. Error test: close underlying fd, verify read/write return appropriate errors. Verify TapAsyncFd is Send+Sync (required for Arc across tasks).

subtasks.title: Integrate RX pipeline components and add demux miss rate limiting
subtasks.description: Wire together buffer pool, RX worker threads, tunnel registry demux, and TAP writer tasks into complete RX pipeline, implement rate-limited logging for demux misses to prevent log spam under attack, and add comprehensive tracing instrumentation
subtasks.details: In src/packet/rx.rs add: (1) pub fn start_rx_pipeline(raw_v4: RawFd, raw_v6: Option<RawFd>, registry: Arc<TunnelRegistry>, pool_size: usize, shutdown: CancellationToken) -> RxPipelineHandle that: creates BufferPool with pool_size, spawns spawn_rx_worker_v4(raw_v4, registry.clone(), pool.clone()), conditionally spawns spawn_rx_worker_v6 if IPv6 socket present, returns RxPipelineHandle { v4_thread: JoinHandle<()>, v6_thread: Option<JoinHandle<()>>, pool: Arc<BufferPool> } for lifecycle management. (2) Implement rate-limited demux miss logging: create static AtomicU64 last_log_time_ms and AtomicU64 miss_count_since_last_log, in demux miss path: increment miss_count, check if now_millis() - last_log_time_ms.load(Relaxed) > 1000 (1 second), if true: log at INFO level with tracing::info!("Demux misses: {} in last second", miss_count_since_last_log.swap(0, Relaxed)), update last_log_time. This prevents log spam from floods while maintaining visibility. (3) Add tracing spans: instrument rx_loop_v4 with tracing::info_span!("rx_worker_v4"), instrument each packet processing with tracing::trace_span!("process_packet", tunnel_id = ?key.tunnel_id, src = ?key.peer_addr). Use tracing::instrument macro on functions. (4) Update TunnelRegistry integration: when tunnel is created, spawn spawn_tap_writer_task and store JoinHandle in TunnelHandle. When tunnel deleted, send shutdown to writer task via CancellationToken and await join. (5) Add graceful shutdown: RxPipelineHandle::shutdown(&self) sends shutdown signal to worker threads (use thread-safe channel or AtomicBool), awaits thread joins with timeout, logs any remaining packets in pool. (6) In src/main.rs integration: after receiving raw socket FDs from helper, call start_rx_pipeline and store handle for cleanup on shutdown. Document that RX workers are OS threads (not tokio tasks) to avoid blocking work-stealing scheduler, but TAP writers are tokio tasks for async I/O integration.
subtasks.testStrategy: End-to-end RX test: (1) Create test setup with mock raw sockets and TAP fds, populate TunnelRegistry with test tunnel, inject EoIP packets into raw socket, verify Ethernet frames appear on TAP fd with correct content. (2) Multi-tunnel test: register 10 tunnels with different (tunnel_id, peer_addr) keys, send 100 packets for each, verify all demuxed correctly. (3) Demux miss rate limiting test: send 10000 packets with unknown tunnel_id over 5 seconds, verify log output contains ~5 INFO messages (one per second) with cumulative miss counts. (4) Performance test: inject 500K pps using packet generator, measure throughput and verify target >500K pps from threading.md. (5) Shutdown test: start pipeline, send packets, trigger shutdown, verify threads join cleanly within 5 seconds. (6) Buffer pool test: run 1M packets through pipeline, verify pool size stable (all buffers returned). (7) Statistics validation: verify per-tunnel rx_packets/rx_bytes counters accurate across multiple tunnels. Run with tracing subscriber to verify instrumentation works. Run with valgrind or ASAN to detect memory leaks.

tasks.title: Implement TX packet path with adaptive batching and raw socket sendmmsg
tasks.description: Build TX pipeline: per-tunnel TAP reader tasks, header encoding with headroom, adaptive batching state machine, shared raw socket sendmmsg for high throughput
tasks.details:
Implement:
- src/packet/tx.rs: spawn_tap_reader_task(tap_fd, tunnel_id, local_addr, remote_addr, tx_channel) using AsyncFd::readable(), read Ethernet frames, encode header into buffer headroom via eoip_proto::encode_*, send PacketBuf to batcher channel
- src/packet/batch.rs: TxBatcher with adaptive FSM:
  - State: Immediate (queue empty) vs Batching (accumulating)
  - Immediate: sendto() single packet, transition to Batching if queue depth > low_water_mark (8)
  - Batching: accumulate up to high_water_mark (256) or batch_timeout_us (50), then sendmmsg(), transition to Immediate if queue drains
- spawn_tx_batcher_v4(raw_sock, rx_channel) and v6 variant
- Use nix::sys::socket::sendmmsg with iovec array, handle EAGAIN/ENOBUFS by dropping packet and incrementing tx_errors

Pre-allocate mmsghdr arrays. Use tokio::time::timeout for batch flush.
tasks.testStrategy: Latency test: single packet, verify <150us RX→TX (Immediate mode). Throughput test: iperf3 TCP, verify adaptive batching achieves 3+ Gbps. Batch FSM test: monitor state transitions under varying load. ENOBUFS handling: verify graceful degradation under socket buffer pressure

subtasks.title: Implement PacketBuf structure with headroom-based buffer allocation
subtasks.description: Create the PacketBuf type in src/packet/mod.rs with pre-allocated headroom for zero-copy header insertion, integrated with crossbeam ArrayQueue-based buffer pool
subtasks.details: In crates/eoip-rs/src/packet/mod.rs: Define struct PacketBuf { data: Box<[u8]>, head: usize, tail: usize, pool: Arc<BufferPool> } with constants HEADER_HEADROOM=64, MAX_FRAME_SIZE=1522, BUF_TOTAL=1586. Implement methods: new() initializing with head=HEADER_HEADROOM, tail=HEADER_HEADROOM; payload_slice() returning &[u8] from head..tail; prepend_header(size: usize) decrementing head and returning mutable slice for header writes; set_payload(offset: usize, len: usize) for RX path. Implement Drop trait returning buffer to pool.pool.push(). Define struct BufferPool { pool: crossbeam::queue::ArrayQueue<PacketBuf>, buf_size: usize } with get() -> Option<PacketBuf> popping from queue, and sizing formula: pool_count = max(256, 64*4 + max_tunnels*2). Document memory layout in doc comments with ASCII diagram showing [headroom|payload|unused] regions and how head pointer moves left during header prepend.
subtasks.testStrategy: Unit tests: (1) Verify buffer allocation initializes with head=64, tail=64, data.len()=1586. (2) Test prepend_header(8) decrements head to 56 and returns 8-byte mutable slice. (3) Test buffer pool exhaustion returns None without panicking. (4) Verify Drop returns buffer to pool by get()->drop->get() cycle. (5) Multi-threaded test with 4 threads getting/returning buffers verifies lock-free pool works correctly.

subtasks.title: Implement per-tunnel TAP reader task with AsyncFd integration
subtasks.description: Create spawn_tap_reader_task in src/packet/tx.rs that spawns a tokio task per tunnel to read Ethernet frames from TAP fd using AsyncFd::readable(), encode headers via eoip_proto::encode_*, and send to batcher channel
subtasks.details: In crates/eoip-rs/src/packet/tx.rs: Implement pub async fn spawn_tap_reader_task(tap_fd: Arc<AsyncFd<OwnedFd>>, tunnel_id: u16, local_addr: IpAddr, remote_addr: IpAddr, tx_channel: crossbeam::channel::Sender<PacketBuf>, buffer_pool: Arc<BufferPool>) -> JoinHandle<()>. Inside spawned task: loop { tap_fd.readable().await?; let mut buf = buffer_pool.get().ok_or_continue(); let n = tap_fd.read(&mut buf.data[HEADER_HEADROOM..])? handling EAGAIN; if n < 14 { continue; /* too short */ } buf.set_payload(HEADER_HEADROOM, n); match local_addr { IpAddr::V4(_) => { buf.prepend_header(8); eoip_proto::gre::encode_eoip_header(tunnel_id, n as u16, &mut buf.payload_slice())?; }, IpAddr::V6(_) => { buf.prepend_header(2); eoip_proto::etherip::encode_eoipv6_header(tunnel_id, &mut buf.payload_slice())?; } }; tx_channel.try_send(buf).unwrap_or_else(|_| stats.tx_errors.fetch_add(1)); }. Handle AsyncFd registration errors. Add tracing::trace! for frame reads (disabled by default).
subtasks.testStrategy: Integration test: Create mock TAP fd using pty/pipe, write synthetic 64-byte Ethernet frame, verify spawn_tap_reader_task reads it and encodes correct EoIP header (magic [0x20,0x01,0x64,0x00] + payload_len=64 BE + tunnel_id LE). Verify buffer headroom is correctly used (head pointer at 56 for 8B GRE, at 62 for 2B EtherIP). Latency test: measure time from write to channel send, target <50us. Error test: verify EAGAIN handled without panicking, verify short frames (<14B) skipped.

subtasks.title: Implement adaptive batching state machine in TxBatcher
subtasks.description: Create src/packet/batch.rs implementing TxBatcher with bimodal FSM: Immediate mode (sendto single packet) when queue empty, Batching mode (accumulate + sendmmsg) when queue depth exceeds low_water_mark, with configurable thresholds
subtasks.details: In crates/eoip-rs/src/packet/batch.rs: Define struct BatchConfig { low_water_mark: usize=8, high_water_mark: usize=256, batch_timeout_us: u64=50 }. Define enum BatchState { Immediate, Batching { batch: ArrayVec<(SocketAddr, PacketBuf), 256>, flush_deadline: Instant } }. Implement struct TxBatcher { state: BatchState, raw_sock: RawFd, config: BatchConfig, stats: Arc<AtomicU64> }. Implement fn process_packet(&mut self, pkt: PacketBuf, remote: SocketAddr, queue_depth: usize) -> Action { match self.state { Immediate if queue_depth <= low_water_mark => sendto_single(pkt), Immediate if queue_depth > low_water_mark => transition to Batching, accumulate pkt, Batching => accumulate pkt; if batch.len() >= high_water_mark { FlushNow } else if queue_depth > 32 { tighten deadline to 10us } else { Wait } } }. Define enum Action { FlushNow, Wait }. Implement flush() calling nix::sys::socket::sendmmsg with iovec array built from batch. Add #[inline] hints on hot path functions.
subtasks.testStrategy: State machine test: (1) Verify initial state is Immediate. (2) Send packet with queue_depth=0, verify immediate sendto() called. (3) Send packets with queue_depth=16, verify transition to Batching after low_water_mark. (4) Accumulate 256 packets, verify sendmmsg() called. (5) Test deadline tightening: queue_depth=64 should set 10us deadline vs 50us default. (6) Verify batch resets to Immediate after flush completes. Benchmark: verify Immediate mode adds <100us latency, Batching mode achieves >3Gbps with iperf3.

subtasks.title: Implement spawn_tx_batcher tasks for IPv4 and IPv6
subtasks.description: Create spawn_tx_batcher_v4 and spawn_tx_batcher_v6 functions in src/packet/batch.rs that spawn dedicated tokio tasks consuming from crossbeam channel, running TxBatcher FSM, and calling sendmmsg on shared raw socket
subtasks.details: In crates/eoip-rs/src/packet/batch.rs: Implement pub fn spawn_tx_batcher_v4(raw_sock: RawFd, rx_channel: crossbeam::channel::Receiver<(SocketAddr, PacketBuf)>, config: BatchConfig) -> JoinHandle<()> { tokio::spawn(async move { let mut batcher = TxBatcher::new(raw_sock, config); loop { tokio::select! { Ok((addr, pkt)) = async { rx_channel.recv() } => { let queue_depth = rx_channel.len(); match batcher.process_packet(pkt, addr, queue_depth) { Action::FlushNow => batcher.flush()?, Action::Wait => {} } }, _ = tokio::time::sleep_until(batcher.flush_deadline()), if batcher.has_pending() => { batcher.flush()?; } } } }) }. Implement spawn_tx_batcher_v6 identically but type-hinted for IPv6 SocketAddr. Use tokio::select! for timeout-driven flushes. Handle EAGAIN/ENOBUFS from sendmmsg by dropping packets and incrementing stats.tx_errors. Implement sendmmsg wrapper using nix::sys::socket::sendmmsg with pre-allocated mmsghdr array (reused across calls).
subtasks.testStrategy: Integration test: (1) Create test raw socket, spawn batcher, send 10 packets via channel, verify sendmmsg called. (2) Send single packet with empty queue, verify sendto() path used (Immediate mode). (3) Send 300 packets rapidly, verify batching triggers at low_water_mark=8. (4) Test timeout: send 5 packets, wait 60us, verify flush triggered by deadline. (5) ENOBUFS test: fill socket buffer, verify graceful degradation (packets dropped, tx_errors incremented, no panic). (6) Benchmark: 4 tunnels sending simultaneously, verify >5Gbps aggregate throughput with iperf3.

subtasks.title: Integrate TX pipeline components with TunnelManager and implement error handling
subtasks.description: Wire up TAP reader tasks and TX batchers in TunnelManager, implement graceful error handling (ENOBUFS, EAGAIN, EMSGSIZE), add per-tunnel stats tracking, and create end-to-end TX path from TAP read to sendmmsg
subtasks.details: In crates/eoip-rs/src/tunnel/mod.rs: Modify TunnelManager to spawn per-tunnel TAP readers during tunnel creation via spawn_tap_reader_task(). Create shared v4/v6 batcher channels: let (v4_tx, v4_rx) = crossbeam::channel::bounded(1024); spawn_tx_batcher_v4(raw_sock_v4, v4_rx, config.batch_config). Store batcher channel senders in TunnelHandle. In TAP reader, send (remote_addr, packet_buf) tuples to appropriate batcher channel. Implement error handling: (1) EMSGSIZE from sendmmsg → log warning with tunnel_id and MTU suggestion, increment tx_errors. (2) ENOBUFS → drop packet silently, increment tx_errors (backpressure). (3) TAP read EIO → tear down tunnel, log critical error. Add metrics: stats.tx_packets.fetch_add(1, Ordering::Relaxed) in batcher after successful sendmmsg. Add stats.last_tx_timestamp.store(now_millis(), Ordering::Relaxed). Create src/packet/mod.rs with pub mod tx; pub mod batch; pub use batch::BatchConfig;. Add tracing::debug! for state transitions (rate-limited to 1/sec). Add graceful shutdown: on CancellationToken signal, abort TAP reader tasks, flush batchers, close channels.
subtasks.testStrategy: End-to-end test: (1) Create tunnel with test TAP fd, write 100 Ethernet frames to TAP, verify all received at mock peer socket with correct EoIP headers. (2) Verify stats: tx_packets==100, tx_bytes==total, last_tx_timestamp within 100ms. (3) MTU test: write 1600-byte frame (exceeds 1500 MTU), verify EMSGSIZE error, tx_errors incremented, frame not sent. (4) Graceful shutdown test: trigger shutdown token, verify TAP tasks abort cleanly, batchers flush pending packets, no panics. (5) Performance test: 100 tunnels, iperf3 across all, verify aggregate >8Gbps on jumbo frames, >3Gbps on 1500 MTU. Verify CPU usage <25% per Gbps with perf stat.

tasks.title: Implement keepalive state machine and end-to-end integration testing

subtasks.title: Implement keepalive manager module with per-tunnel state tracking
subtasks.description: Create src/keepalive.rs with KeepaliveManager that spawns per-tunnel tasks to send zero-payload EoIP packets at configured intervals and track tunnel health state transitions
subtasks.details:
Implement KeepaliveManager in crates/eoip-rs/src/keepalive.rs:
- Define KeepaliveState enum: Active, Stale with atomic transitions

- Transition back to Active on RX resume (last_rx updates)
- Cancel task on tunnel teardown using CancellationToken
- Use tracing for state transitions with tunnel_id context
subtasks.testStrategy: Unit tests: verify zero-payload packet encoding matches protocol spec ([0x20,0x01,0x64,0x00,0x00,0x00,TID_lo,TID_hi]). Mock time tests: verify Stale transition after timeout. subtasks.title: Update RX packet path to handle zero-payload keepalive packets

subtasks.details:
Update spawn_rx_worker_v4 and spawn_rx_worker_v6 in crates/eoip-rs/src/packet/rx.rs:
- After header decode, check payload_len field (EoIP) or computed frame length (EoIPv6)
- If payload_len == 0: update stats.last_rx timestamp, increment stats.rx_keepalive counter, skip TAP delivery, continue to next packet
- If payload_len > 0: proceed with existing TAP write logic
- Add new AtomicU64 field rx_keepalive to TunnelStats struct in crates/eoip-rs/src/stats.rs
- Ensure validation still checks magic bytes and tunnel ID for keepalive packets
- Update eoip-proto validation functions to explicitly allow zero-length payloads
subtasks.testStrategy: RX test with crafted keepalive: send 8-byte EoIP packet with payload_len=0, verify stats.last_rx updates but no TAP write. Verify stats.rx_keepalive increments. Mixed traffic test: interleave data frames and keepalives, verify both processed correctly. Malformed keepalive rejection test

subtasks.title: Integrate keepalive into tunnel lifecycle state machine
subtasks.description: Wire keepalive manager into tunnel creation/destruction flow, ensuring Active state spawns keepalive task and Stale state stops TX while maintaining RX demux entry
subtasks.details:
Update crates/eoip-rs/src/tunnel/lifecycle.rs:
- In transition to ACTIVE state: call keepalive::spawn_keepalive_task(), store CancellationToken in TunnelHandle
- Subscribe keepalive state changes: when KeepaliveManager signals Stale, transition tunnel to STALE state
- In STALE state: cancel TAP reader task (stop TX), keep tunnel entry in DemuxTable (preserve RX)
- On keepalive recovery (last_rx updates within timeout): transition STALE → ACTIVE, respawn TAP reader and keepalive tasks
- In TEARING_DOWN: cancel keepalive task, remove from DemuxTable, close TAP fd
- Update TunnelHandle struct to include keepalive_cancel: Option<CancellationToken>
- Emit tracing events for all state transitions with tunnel_id, peer_addr, state fields
subtasks.testStrategy: Lifecycle test: create tunnel, verify Active spawns keepalive. Stale simulation: mock timeout, verify TX stops but RX continues. Recovery test: resume RX traffic, verify Active restoration. Teardown test: verify keepalive task cancelled and no resource leaks. State machine invariant tests using proptest

Create tests/integration/eoip_pair.rs:
